text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic small bowel resection for a small bowel GI stromal tumor. This was actually incidentally found on a CT scan done to evaluate diverticulitis. It was a patient's first attack. He recovered well from the diverticulitis. There was no indication for surgery after that one benign attack, and we decided to go after the small bowel mass, which at the time was presumed to be a GI stromal tumor and final pathology proved that it was. I have not done many robotic small bowel resections. I chose to do this case as a robotic case.

Hello, I'm sharing a robotic small bowel resection for a small bowel GI stromal tumor. This was actually incidentally found on a CT scan done to evaluate diverticulitis. It was a patient's first attack. He recovered well from the diverticulitis. There was no indication for surgery after that one benign attack, and we decided to go after the small bowel mass, which at the time was presumed to be a GI stromal tumor and final pathology proved that it was. I have not done many robotic small bowel resections. I chose to do this case as a robotic case.

The surgeon positions the robotic ports in a low transverse orientation, with arm number two located suprapubically, preparing to upsize for the extraction incision. Using Firefly mode, the surgeon visually assesses the mesentery for negative margins and employs a stapler to divide the mesenteric tissue.

The surgeon positions the ports in a low transverse orientation, with arm number two located suprapubically, preparing to upsize for the extraction incision. A 12mm port is used. The mesentery is taken to ensure grossly negative margins, and a rim of the mesentery is excised. In firefly mode, the stapler is introduced to divide the margins.
I feel like this case went well. It took under fifty minutes in total OR time. I think it was definitely facilitated by the dexterity and manipulation of the articulating arms.

The surgeon lines up the antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis, creating enterotomies in the bowel. A stay suture is placed on the near side to assist in pulling the bowel onto the vascular load stapler, which is inserted through the suprapubic port.

The surgeon lines up the antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis and creates enterotomies. A stay suture is placed on the near side to assist in pulling the bowel onto the vascular load stapler through the suprapubic port.

The surgeon cuts the stay suture and begins closing the common channel using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, starting at the near corner with an over-and-over stitch technique. The suture is then used to imbricate the tissue as the surgeon stitches back down.

The surgeon cuts the stay suture and begins to close the common channel using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, starting at the near corner and performing an over-and-over stitch. The suture is then used to imbricate the tissue as the surgeon stitches back down.

Final pathology was a GI stromal tumor, very low grade, presented at our tumor board and was deemed adequate for surveillance, no need for adjuvant medical therapy. So again, just a over and over stitch. out to the far corner.

Final pathology was a GI stromal tumor, very low grade, presented at our tumor board and was deemed adequate for surveillance, no need for adjuvant medical therapy. So again, just a over and over stitch. out to the far corner.

The surgeon begins imbricating the bowel, using a single-layer canal suture technique to close the enterotomy, ensuring no exposed puckering mucosa. The suture covers the near corner completely, creating two layers throughout the closure. A knot is tied to secure the suture.

The surgeon imbricates the enterotomy closure using a single-layer canal suture technique, ensuring no exposed puckering mucosa and covering the near corner for a complete two-layer closure. The anastomosis is covered with omentum and buried beneath the transverse colon. The specimen is extracted through the suprapubic 12mm port site.
The surgeon covers the anastomosis with omentum, burying it beneath the transverse colon. The specimen is extracted through the suprapubic port site.
